questions.question: Why does the label "history of Israel" fail to account fully for the Pentateuch?

options.text: Because the Pentateuch contains no historical narrative
Because large sections before Abraham and Exodus precede Israel’s emergence
Because Israel is never mentioned in Genesis
Because the Pentateuch is only legal material

questions.explanation: The lectures stressed that Genesis 1–11 and other early material come before Israel formally emerges, so "history of Israel" is too narrow.

questions.question: Why is "God" considered too broad as a one-word summary of the Pentateuch?

options.text: Because God is absent from the Pentateuch
Because it is false but helpful
Because it is true but not specific enough to guide interpretation
Because it only applies to Deuteronomy

questions.explanation: The lecture acknowledged that "God" is true, but too general to function as a strong interpretive description of the Pentateuch.

questions.question: What concern did the lecture raise about the title "Old Testament"?

options.text: It is the Hebrew title used by Moses
It may lead readers to think the text is obsolete or outdated
It proves the canon is Roman Catholic
It appears as a self-designation throughout the OT

questions.explanation: The lecture argued that the word "old" can bias readers into thinking the Old Testament is obsolete or less valuable.

questions.question: In the lecture, what point was made about the word "testament" in "Old Testament"?

options.text: It is unrelated to covenant
It is a Greek term meaning prophecy
It comes through Latin usage and means covenant
It refers only to the Ten Commandments

questions.explanation: The lecture explained that "testament" comes through Latin usage and corresponds to the idea of covenant.

questions.question: According to the lecture, what does inerrancy need to be defined in terms of?

options.text: What later commentators infer from Scripture
What Scripture affirms
What church councils declared
What modern science can verify

questions.explanation: The lecture tied inerrancy carefully to what Scripture affirms, not to careless or overly broad claims.

questions.question: What does the lecture say inspiration does NOT eliminate?

options.text: Human authorship, personality, and literary intention
The authority of church tradition
The need for translation
The existence of canon questions

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasized that God used real human authors with purpose, style, and literary strategy under divine supervision.

questions.question: What was the lecture’s main critique of reading David and Goliath mainly as "your giants"?

options.text: It is too historical
It makes the passage primarily about the modern reader rather than the author’s purpose
It denies that David existed
It ignores all application

questions.explanation: The lecture’s concern was that moralizing takes the interpretive key away from the author and turns the passage mainly into self-application.

questions.question: According to the lecture, what is the basic hermeneutical principle for interpreting genre?

options.text: Modern assumptions determine the meaning of ancient texts
The nature of the material determines the rules of interpretation
Every genre should be read the same way
Narrative should always be moralized

questions.explanation: The lecture stated that the nature of the material determines the interpretive rules, so readers must attend to genre and ancient conventions.

questions.question: What observation by Jean Astruc helped launch source-critical study of Genesis?

options.text: The use of poetry in Genesis 1
Alternating divine names such as Elohim and Yahweh
The absence of genealogies
The presence of legal material in Deuteronomy

questions.explanation: Astruc noted divine-name patterns and proposed Moses used earlier sources, which later fed source criticism.

questions.question: Who systematized the Documentary Hypothesis into the classic JEDP model discussed in the lecture?

options.text: E.D. Hirsch
Julius Wellhausen
John Sailhamer
Rolf Rendtorff

questions.explanation: The lecture identified Julius Wellhausen as the major figure who formalized JEDP in its classic form.

questions.question: In the classic Documentary Hypothesis, which source is commonly linked to Josiah’s reforms and the law book discovery?

options.text: J
E
D
P

questions.explanation: The lecture associated D, the Deuteronomist source, with Josiah’s reforms and the discovered law book.

questions.question: What is the main focus of form criticism as described in the lecture?

options.text: Finding final canonical unity only
Identifying recurring forms and inferring their original Sitz im Leben
Rejecting all oral tradition
Dating every text to the exile

questions.explanation: Form criticism studies repeated forms and tries to infer their original social setting or Sitz im Leben.

questions.question: What limitation of form criticism did the lecture stress?

options.text: It cannot read Hebrew poetry
There is no simple line from a reconstructed original setting to the final written text
It only applies to the New Testament
It ignores oral culture completely

questions.explanation: The lecture stressed that many stages may intervene between a reconstructed setting and the final written form.

questions.question: What does tradition criticism focus on?

options.text: How smaller units were transmitted and combined into larger complexes
Only the final canonical order
Modern church usage of biblical texts
The doctrine of inerrancy only

questions.explanation: Tradition criticism studies the transmission and growth of smaller units into larger narrative or literary complexes.

questions.question: What does canon criticism emphasize?

options.text: Only hypothetical earlier sources
The authority and significance of the Bible’s final canonical shape
That book order never affects interpretation
That councils created the canon’s authority

questions.explanation: Canon criticism focuses on the authoritative final form and how the canonical arrangement shapes meaning.

questions.question: How did the lecture define authorship in connection with composition criticism?

options.text: Copying earlier texts without purpose
Selecting, arranging, adapting, and writing material into a meaningful whole
Rejecting all prior sources
Using only oral recitation

questions.explanation: The lecture described authorship compositionally: authors select, arrange, adapt, and write material into a structured whole.
